text: Saguaro National Park
A monarch. The Saguaro,
king of all cactuses, and the royal icon of the American Southwest.
Here in the Sonoran Desert is the only place on earth where the Saguaro grows.
The home of Saguaro National Park, near the Arizona -Mexican border,
there are 91 ,000 protected acres bisected by the city of Tucson.
In this park, the saguaro is a thirsty monarch, sucking up as much as 200
gallons of water during a rare but torrential desert rainstorm.
These royal giants grow slowly, but can reach 50 feet in height
and reign through a lifespan of 150 years.
Solid as they appear to be, it is water that makes up most of their weight.
They are the most recognized cactus in the world.
The saguaro has become a symbol of the American West.
Park visitors head out on the Cactus Forest Trail, a desert thoroughfare much used by hikers and bicycles.
Hikers must avoid brushing against the spines of the low -growing, shovel -like Cholla cactus.
They bristle with barbed spines, which are painfully difficult to remove once embedded in your flesh.
Many desert plants bristle. The agave.
Water is so scarce that cactuses protect the water they have stored with sharp spines.
The prickly pear. Without these spines, insects, birds, and animals would eat them.
At first glance, this expansive desert seems lifeless.
Too much sun. Too little rain.
But a longer look reveals a profusion of wildlife.
constant scarcity of water, each creature struggles endlessly to stay alive. Water is life.
The wriggle saguaro provides sweet fruit for hungry creatures and homes to birds,
such as the Harris Hawk and the elf owl, the smallest owl in existence.
Like all deserts, Saguaro has extreme fluctuations in daily temperatures.
Cloudless skies and unshaded ground make a blast furnace of the daytime.
While at night, without cloud cover or moisture to hold the heat, bitter cold takes over.
Some creatures burrow underground or crawl into rock dens to escape the killer sun of summer and the cold nights of winter.
When summer temperatures soar to over 100 degrees Fahrenheit,
scorpions exude a coating of wax to seal in moisture, an effective barrier against dehydration.
The only known year -round spring is found at King Canyon in the Tucson Mountains.
This reliable source of water is often frequented by the collared peccary,
a harried wild mammal. They're also known by their Spanish name, javelina.
When not sleeping during the heat of day, these members of the New World pig family are found rooting among beds of prickly
pear. They're social animals, usually traveling in herds.
The king of cactus and profuse wildlife aren't the park's only attractions.
Red Hills Visitor Center in the Tucson Mountain District offers an insight to the Native American take on the saguaro cactus.
Signal Hill is well worth the hike. Here, some thousand years ago,
near what is today an inviting picnic area, Native Americans left hundreds of petroglyphs.
Though we have never deciphered the meaning of these pictorial records, we feel certain that the prehistoric people who made them
hiked in these forests of the Saguaro and groves of Mesquite, probing the secrets of the desert as we do today.
Thus, this seemingly dead earth reveals itself to be a land of life.
